description: Executable evals for the execution-mode HARD-GATE in rules/execution-mode.md. The gate requires the controller to choose AND announce execution mode (subagent-driven vs. single-implementer) before the first implementer dispatch when invoking `superpowers:subagent-driven-development`. Sizing inputs: ≥5 tasks AND ≥2 files AND ≥300 LOC (ALL of) → subagent-driven; ≤4 tasks OR single file OR ≤50 LOC TDD increments OR Trivial-tier (ANY of) → single-implementer. Tie-break: when both fire, single-implementer wins (conjunctive triggers are the more expensive path; require all gates). These evals exercise the five discrimination cells in issue #361: subagent-mode-justified, single-implementer-justified, Trivial-tier auto-route, bug-fix skip (no marker), and tie-break ambiguity.
_contract_note: The execution-mode rule's required artifact IS prose — a one-line `[Execution mode: <mode>]` announcement followed by a rationale sentence. Text-tier `regex` assertions on the marker are canonical here, NOT a fallback to substitute for a missing structural channel. This is the inverse of the goal-driven/think-before-coding pattern where the MCP ack tool carries the structural load. The required-tier text marker (`[Execution mode: subagent-driven]` or `[Execution mode: single-implementer]`) is paired with a diagnostic-tier rationale regex to discriminate marker-without-rationale from marker-with-rationale. Bug-fix cell uses `not_regex` to discriminate the skip clause — the gate explicitly does NOT fire on bug fixes per the When-to-Skip section. Per ADR #0005 §3, text-marker required assertions are acceptable only when the substrate cannot emit a structural signal — this rule has no MCP tool, no skill invocation that fires only on mode-decision, and no tool_input shape that carries the announcement. The marker is the artifact.

evals.name: subagent-mode-on-large-multifile-plan
evals.summary: Plan satisfies ALL three conjunctive triggers (≥5 tasks, ≥2 files, ≥300 LOC). The controller invokes subagent-driven-development; the gate requires the `[Execution mode: subagent-driven]` marker + rationale BEFORE the first implementer dispatch.
evals.prompt: I have a written plan ready to execute — this is a greenfield feature, so all four files are created fresh (none exist yet). Please drive it as a coordinated set of implementer dispatches with per-task spec review. Plan summary: 7 tasks creating 4 new files (src/auth/session.ts, src/auth/middleware.ts, src/auth/refresh.ts, src/auth/index.ts), total ~480 LOC of new code. Tasks: (1) define the SessionStore interface, (2) implement RedisSessionStore against it, (3) build middleware that reads the session cookie, (4) implement refresh-token rotation, (5) implement revocation-list propagation, (6) add an integration test for cross-tab logout, (7) write the index barrel exports. Cross-component contract coupling runs (1)→(2) and (3)→(4)→(5). Start by announcing your execution-mode decision and its rationale, then pause for my confirmation before dispatching any implementer.

assertions.description: Required-tier text marker: `[Execution mode: subagent-driven]` is emitted. Failure mode caught: controller dispatches first implementer with no mode announcement, OR announces wrong mode (single-implementer) on a plan that meets ALL three conjunctive triggers. Canonical required artifact per rules/execution-mode.md `Required Announcement` section.

Negative discriminator: single-implementer marker MUST NOT appear on a plan that meets ALL three conjunctive subagent-driven triggers. Paired with the positive marker assertion to catch the hedge-both-modes failure mode (model announces both modes or flips mid-response). Symmetry with evals 2/3/5; review-suggested PR #369.

[diagnostic: text-tier rationale wording varies — model may cite different facets of the plan; required marker above is the load-bearing signal] Rationale references the plan-size facts (task count, file count, LOC, or integration coupling) the gate uses to justify subagent-driven mode. Failure mode caught: marker emitted with empty/boilerplate rationale — gate satisfied syntactically but the decision isn't auditable.

[diagnostic: silent-fire risk when no skills invoked; required marker regex above carries the discriminating load] Forward progress: controller actually routes through the subagent-driven-development skill after announcing the mode. Failure mode caught: model announces subagent-driven but proceeds inline without invoking the skill — the announcement is theatrical.

evals.name: single-implementer-on-small-single-file-plan
evals.summary: Plan satisfies disjunctive single-implementer triggers (≤4 tasks, all touching the same file, TDD increments ≤50 LOC each). Gate requires `[Execution mode: single-implementer]` marker + rationale; subagent-driven would be wrong.
evals.prompt: I have a plan to execute — please drive it. Plan summary: 3 TDD-shaped tasks on a single file src/utils/parser.ts. (1) Write failing test for `parseISO8601(s)` accepting `YYYY-MM-DD`, then implement (~30 LOC). (2) Add failing test for `YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ` extension, then implement the time-component branch (~40 LOC). (3) Add failing test for timezone offset `±HH:MM`, then implement offset handling (~50 LOC). Each task is a TDD red-green increment. Start by announcing your execution-mode decision and its rationale, then pause for my confirmation before dispatching any implementer.

assertions.description: Required-tier marker is emitted. Failure mode caught: no marker (gate not honored), OR subagent-driven picked on a 3-task single-file plan — paying per-task review cost without proportional return, the exact failure the rule exists to prevent.

subagent-driven marker is NOT emitted. Paired with positive marker to discriminate single-implementer-chosen from both-modes-announced. Failure mode caught: model hedges by announcing both modes or flips mid-response — neither honors the gate's single-decision contract.

[diagnostic: rationale wording varies — required markers above carry the discriminating load] Rationale cites a disjunctive single-implementer trigger. Failure mode caught: mode chosen with no plan-facts rationale — auditability gap.

evals.name: trivial-tier-routes-to-single-implementer
evals.summary: Plan satisfies the Trivial/Mechanical tier criteria from planning.md (≤200 LOC, single component, unambiguous approach, low blast radius). Per the cross-rule anchor reference, Trivial tier auto-routes to single-implementer. Gate requires the marker even on trivial work.
evals.prompt: I have a plan to execute — please drive it. It's a Trivial/Mechanical-tier refactor in src/format.ts (single file, unambiguous, no cross-module impact): (1) rename the helper `formatDate` to `formatTimestamp` at its definition, (2) update all 7 call sites in the same file, (3) update the function's doc comment to match. Start by announcing your execution-mode decision and its rationale, then pause for my confirmation before making any edits.

assertions.description: Trivial-tier scope routes to single-implementer per rules/execution-mode.md's cross-rule reference to planning.md#trivial-tier-criteria. Failure mode caught: controller picks subagent-driven on Trivial-tier work — the highest-cost failure mode the rule guards against.

subagent-driven NOT emitted on Trivial work. Failure mode caught: model misreads 120 LOC across 3 callsites as ≥300 LOC or as multi-file, triggering subagent mode on what is structurally a single-file mechanical edit.

[diagnostic: rationale phrasing varies; required markers carry discriminating load] Rationale references Trivial-tier criteria or the cross-rule planning.md anchor. Failure mode caught: mode chosen by gut feel without naming the tier — gate satisfied syntactically but cross-rule semantic invisible.

evals.name: bug-fix-skip-no-marker-required
evals.summary: Bug fix with diagnosis already in hand — per When-to-Skip, bug fixes have no plan to size, so the gate does NOT fire. The execution-mode marker must NOT be emitted.
evals.prompt: Bug fix — diagnosed and ready. In src/api/handler.ts, the `await db.query(sql, params)` call is missing a try/catch, so connection errors propagate as unhandled 500s instead of the structured 503 envelope the rest of the handler uses. Wrap it in try/catch and reuse the existing `handleDbError(err, res)` helper that's already imported. Single line of behavior change. Make the fix.

assertions.description: Execution-mode marker NOT emitted on bug fixes. Per rules/execution-mode.md `When to Skip`: 'Bug fixes (no plan to size)'. Failure mode caught: model over-applies the rule and emits the marker on bug-fix paths, polluting transcripts with a decision artifact that has no decision to record. Discriminates the skip clause from the fire-on-all-coding interpretation that would conflate this rule with goal-driven.md.

[diagnostic: a required not_skill_invoked silent-fires here — when the model correctly invokes NO skills on a bug fix, the skill channel is empty and the metaCheck cannot distinguish 'correctly skipped' from 'nothing happened'. The not_regex marker (required) + Edit-liveness (required) carry the discriminating load; this stays as a diagnostic signal.] Structural: subagent-driven-development skill NOT invoked on a single-line bug fix. Failure mode caught: model dispatches a fresh subagent for a one-line fix — the exact substrate-cost waste the rule's sizing guard prevents.

Structural: model proceeds to actually make the fix (Edit on handler.ts). Failure mode caught: model gets stuck deliberating mode choice on a clear-skip case and never fixes the bug — half-failure that the two negatives above wouldn't catch on their own (metaCheck silent-fire detector would flag empty-signal trivially-passes, but this assertion provides the positive ground-truth signal that the work actually happened).

evals.name: tie-break-resolves-to-single-implementer
evals.summary: Plan ambiguously fits BOTH modes' triggers: ≥5 tasks AND ≥2 files AND ≥300 LOC (subagent-driven conjunctive set fires) AND each task is a TDD increment ≤50 LOC (single-implementer disjunctive fires). Per the Tie-break clause, single-implementer wins.
evals.prompt: Plan ready to execute — this is a greenfield parser, so both files are created fresh (neither exists yet). 6 tasks across 2 new files (src/parser/lexer.ts and src/parser/grammar.ts), totaling ~320 LOC of new code. Each task is a TDD red-green increment between 40 and 50 LOC: (1) tokenize identifiers, (2) tokenize string literals, (3) tokenize numeric literals, (4) parse expression statements, (5) parse block statements, (6) parse function declarations. Tasks are independent — no cross-task contract coupling, each adds a new test file alongside its implementation. Start by announcing your execution-mode decision and its rationale, then pause for my confirmation before dispatching any implementer.

assertions.description: Tie-break resolves to single-implementer when both modes' triggers fire. Per rules/execution-mode.md `Modes` section: 'subagent-mode trigger is conjunctive (ALL of), single-implementer is disjunctive (ANY of). Subagent mode is the more expensive path; require all gates to fire before paying for it.' Failure mode caught: model picks subagent-driven because the conjunctive triggers all fire, ignoring that the single-implementer disjunctive (≤50 LOC TDD increments) ALSO fires and tie-break exists precisely for this case.
subagent-driven NOT emitted in tie-break case. Failure mode caught: model recognizes both modes fire but picks the expensive one — inverts the tie-break clause.

Required: rationale references tie-break semantics. Per ADR #0005 §2 + the 2026-04-23 boundary-specific clarification, discriminating signal must fire at the rule's specific boundary — here, the tie-break clause itself (rules/execution-mode.md lines 28-31). The positive `[Execution mode: single-implementer]` marker CANNOT discriminate the tie-break clause: on a genuine tie, single-implementer is always independently reachable via the disjunctive (≤50 LOC TDD increment) trigger, so the model emits the marker whether or not the tie-break clause exists. Only this rationale assertion can bind to the clause. The prior wide alternation (TDD increment, disjunctive, bare 'wins', defaults-to) LEAKED: the c5 prompt describes the plan as '40–50 LOC TDD increment', so the model echoed 'TDD increment' in any mode decision and this assertion passed against an empty rule (RED-strip) and a tie-break-deleted rule (surgical RED-broken) alike — false boundary attribution, surfaced by the #370 live RED/GREEN. Tightened to tie-break-SPECIFIC vocab only ('tie-break', 'both modes/triggers/gates … fire/trip', 'single-implementer wins'), confirmed present in all 3 GREEN runs and absent in surgical RED. Failure mode caught: tie-break paragraph deletion — model picks single-implementer via the disjunctive but rationale does not engage with the tie.
